---
title: ""
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: right
    echo: false
    css: styles/tables.css
    embed-resources: true
    self-contained-math: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# IBD Patient face higher Incidence of Anal Dysplasia and Cancer, Hightened by HPV, Perianal Disease

*Introduction:* Patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) have increased rates of anal cancer. We evaluated whether high-risk HPV-positive results from anal sampling or perianal disease was associated with higher anal cancer or dysplasia prevalence in IBD.

Methods: We performed a retrospective analysis of Epic Cosmos, an anonymized database of 305,065,760 patients. Patients with active diagnoses from 6/1990 to 6/2026 were stratified as Crohn’s disease, ulcerative colitis, or non-IBD and compared for anal cancer/dysplasia, anal HR-HPV+ DNA, and perianal disease.

Anal HR-HPV+ status, anal cancer/dysplasia, and perianal disease were defined by ICD-10 codes. Anal cancer/dysplasia included HGSIL of anus, anal intraepithelial neoplasia III, or dysplasia of anus. Perianal disease included perianal fistula, abscess, or fissure. Chi-square testing used p \< 0.05. Although the source population included approximately 2.4 million IBD patients, testing denominators, negative results, screening frequency, and HPV vaccination status were unavailable; findings may reflect screening, surveillance, or coding differences.

*Results:*

| IBD Type | \% with Anal HR-HPV+ DNA | \% of population with Anal Cancer | \% with Anal HR-HPV+ DNA, then found to have Anal cancer within 5 years | \% with Perianal Disease, anal cancer within 5 years |
|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| Non-IBD (n=305,065,760) | 0.0075% | 0.04% | 0.003% | 0.003% |
| Crohn's Disease (n=1,206,873) | 0.016% | 0.21% | 0.008% | 0.046% |
| Ulcerative Colitis (n=1,191,972) | 0.027% | 0.27% | 0.013% | 0.04% |

\*=Chi square independence

For anal cancer or dysplasia, 0.04% (p, Z) of non-IBD patients were diagnosed, while 0.21% (p,z) of Crohn's patients and 0.27% of UC patients were diagnosed.

For HPV, 516 IBD patients had HR-HPV+ sampling in the anal area, .

Compared with the general population, patients with IBD had a roughly 5-fold higher prevalence of anal cancer in Crohn’s disease and 7-fold higher prevalence in ulcerative colitis.

Compared with the general population, anal HR-HPV+ DNA prevalence was approximately 2.1-fold higher in Crohn’s disease and 3.6-fold higher in ulcerative colitis. Chi Square analysis rejected independence with a p \<0.001, supporting a significant association between group status and outcome frequency.

Among patients with anal HR-HPV+ DNA, anal cancer diagnosis within 5 years was also more frequent in IBD groups: approximately 2.7-fold higher in Crohn’s disease and 4.3-fold higher in ulcerative colitis compared with the general population. Similarly, among patients with perianal disease, anal cancer diagnosis within 5 years was approximately 15-fold higher in Crohn’s disease and 13-fold higher in ulcerative colitis.

Overall, ulcerative colitis showed the highest prevalence of anal cancer and anal HR-HPV+ DNA, while perianal disease was more strongly associated in Crohn’s disease. Among the studied risk factors, perianal disease showed the largest relative increase in subsequent anal cancer diagnosis.

*Conclusion:* Compared with the general population, patients with IBD had significantly higher observed prevalence of anal cancer and anal HR-HPV+ DNA. Prior perianal disease was associated with an approximately 13- to 15-fold higher frequency of anal cancer diagnosis within 5 years. These findings may support further study of targeted HPV vaccination and screening strategies in IBD patients, particularly those with a history of perianal disease.

Conclusion: Patients with IBD had higher observed prevalence of anal dysplasia, cancer and anal HR-HPV+ DNA compared with the general population, with the highest rates seen in ulcerative colitis. Anal HR-HPV+ DNA and perianal disease were both associated with increased anal cancer diagnosis within 5 years, with perianal disease showing the largest relative increase, particularly in Crohn’s disease. These findings suggest that IBD patients, especially those with anal samples that are HR-HPV+ or with prior perianal disease, may represent a higher-risk group for targeted anal cancer screening strategies.

Fix:\
- Results: include numbers, P values, CI. Make prevalence claims within the conclusion.\
- title - cleaner\
- Methods: \*2.4 million patients were not tested, only the screenings that came positive are available/shown. redefine grouups (as not all patients were tested for DNA, etc.)\
- INCIDENCE -\
- further study - how many were tested vs non, vaccinated vs not, . Part that is not expected, you see this increase in \*
